name: Thermodynamics & Statistical Mechanics
description: Entropy, statistical mechanics, phase transitions, and the arrow of time. The laws that govern heat, work, and the statistical behavior of large ensembles of particles. Thermodynamics is the physics of the many — where microscopic rules produce macroscopic inevitability.
axioms: Zeroth law: thermal equilibrium is transitive (defines temperature)
First law: energy is conserved (dU = δQ - δW)
Second law: entropy of an isolated system never decreases (ΔS ≥ 0)
Third law: entropy approaches zero as T → 0 (Nernst theorem — absolute zero is unattainable)
Boltzmann: S = k_B ln(Ω) — entropy is a measure of the number of accessible microstates
Clausius inequality: δQ/T ≤ dS for any process
Gibbs: the thermodynamic free energies (F, G, H) determine spontaneity under different constraints
reasoning_patterns: State function analysis: identify which quantities depend only on state, not path (U, S, F, G, H)
Maxwell relations: derive partial derivative identities from exactness of thermodynamic potentials
Carnot efficiency: the maximum work extractable between two temperatures is η = 1 - T_c/T_h
Ensemble method: microcanonical (fixed E,V,N), canonical (fixed T,V,N), grand canonical (fixed T,V,μ)
Phase diagram analysis: identify coexistence curves, critical points, triple points, order parameters
Mean-field approximation: replace many-body interactions with an effective field to solve phase transitions
Chemical reaction equilibrium: use Gibbs free energy to find equilibrium composition
boundary_conditions: Does not cover heat transfer mechanisms (conduction/convection/radiation — engineering domain)
Statistical mechanics assumes the ergodic hypothesis (time average = ensemble average), not always valid
Third law has exceptions in disordered systems (glasses, frustrated systems)
Non-equilibrium thermodynamics requires extended frameworks (Onsager, Prigogine)
The arrow of time is statistical, not fundamental — microscopic equations are time-reversible
key_references: Callen, H.B. Thermodynamics and an Introduction to Thermostatistics
Landau & Lifshitz. Statistical Physics (Course of Theoretical Physics Vol.5)
Jaynes, E.T. (1957). Information Theory and Statistical Mechanics
Onsager, L. (1931). Reciprocal Relations in Irreversible Processes
canonical_truths: Entropy is a state function — path independent
Perpetual motion machines of both kinds are impossible
Carnot efficiency η = 1 - T_c/T_h is the theoretical maximum for any heat engine
Phase transitions are classified by discontinuities in derivatives of free energy (Ehrenfest classification)
The arrow of time is encoded in the second law, not in fundamental time-symmetric equations
Boltzmann's constant k_B ≈ 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K connects microscopic and macroscopic scales
